data.title: High Demand, Low Supply: The Bullish Case For Commodities
data.description:
Commodities like gold, silver and copper had a strong run in 2025. Hussein Allidina, Head of Commodities with TD Asset Management, says simple supply-demand dynamics could push prices even higher in 2026.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: While many commodities had a strong run in 2025, our featured guest today says simple supply and demand dynamics could push prices even higher in 2026. Hussein Allidina, managing director and head of commodities with TD Asset Management, joins us now with more.

Hussein, great to see you. We all know about the year behind us and how strong it was. Let's talk about the conditions right now and why you still see them as positive for commodities.

Hussein Allidina: Thanks, Greg. And sorry I'm not in the studio with you today. It's very much the story about where we are in the commodity cycle. 12 to 13 years, the commodity space has not attracted capital into the supply side, frankly. And that has contributed to limiting our ability to produce. And demand is growing.

And in 2026, with the reflation trade very much in focus, demand looks like it's going to continue to grow. And that continues to tighten balances and support prices. Ultimately, we need to see prices move to levels that encourage the companies to deploy capital. And because commodity supply is inelastic, it's going to take years before that copper production, that oil production comes to market.

Greg Bonnell: I know depending on the commodity, we'll have a different story about what the demand is. But overall, are we talking about a stronger global economy than we thought, new technologies, a bit of hedging? What's the play here?

Hussein Allidina: So the biggest driver of commodity demand, broadly speaking, is economic growth. And with this material focus on ensuring that economic growth not just in the US, but globally, is increasing to make the debt and deficit relative to GDP improve, there's a pronounced focus from both a monetary and a fiscal impulse perspective to drive growth higher.

So that's the big thematic. But if we look under the hood, clearly the desire to build out hyperscalers, data centers, and the required power generation is going to be very constructive for commodities, broadly speaking. And the spend on military and defense is very commodity-intensive. So, I'd argue that your GDP being positive is the biggest driver. And then, in the details, there's very specific things that are going to require a tremendous amount of copper, uranium, natural gas as we move into this new world.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, let's get into those specific things. You mentioned copper. Let's talk about the bullish case for copper.

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so copper has moved pretty meaningfully. We're trading north of $13,000 a ton in the LME. Part of this is a reflection of concerns around Section 232 and the magnitude of tariffs, if at all, that we'll see on copper, aluminum, silver, and other strategic materials. That's contributed to bringing supply-- or sucking supply from the rest of the world into the US.

There was some news overnight on Section 232. It doesn't look like a tremendous amount of clarity. But outside of that, Greg, again, GDP growth is very, very supportive for copper demand. And the AI, energy transition theme requires quite a bit of copper.

These hyperscalers, data centers have copper within them. The power that is going to be used to facilitate the growth in data centers requires a tremendous amount of copper. We're seeing aluminum perform as well because there is some industrial substitution that happens as copper prices move higher. But that's the big story on the demand side.

And then on the supply side, we have seen in 2025 a number of mines disappoint. And again, because we've not encouraged CapEx over the course of the last 10 years, when we look at that pipeline in the next three, five, seven years, there's no new major copper projects that are coming to market. And that ultimately, I think, is what lends to this view that the copper market, on a multi-year view, is going to be in deficit.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, that's copper. Let's talk about gold and silver. Gold has been very interesting for a long time, but silver in the last several months, too, catching a lot of attention. What's the story here? What's the setup?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so I'll talk to gold first. And the gold story is very much portfolio diversification on the part of central banks. If we look at emerging market central banks, they have been large buyers of gold, broadly speaking since 2021, following the confiscation of Afghan and then, following that, Russian reserves by the US government.

If you are an emerging market central bank sitting on 80%, 90% of your reserves in fiat, in paper, I think there is some desire to diversify into gold. And we're seeing that. Those emerging market central banks have been buying over 1,000 tons of gold per annum. For perspective, Greg, we produce about 2,500 tons of gold annually. So that's been a big source of demand. And we don't think that that changes anytime in the near future.

And then you have, obviously, seen some interest on the part of institutional and retail to participate in gold. And you can see that by looking at the amount of open interest that sits in the ETFs that track gold. Those ETFs are still well below-- from a open interest perspective, well below the highs that we've seen in recent years. So there is still, I think, some additional fodder there.

Silver-- a very different story. Silver is not being bought by central banks. It is being bought by investors, for sure. There's a larger industrial component to silver. But if I look at the balance, the silver balance, it's in deficit. And I'm going to sound like a broken record, but I'm not encouraged. The production of silver-- if we look at the largest producer of silver in the world, Mexico, their production is declining. And it's going to take time for us to grow supply in a meaningful way.

Now, I just want to caution-- silver is something that has moved meaningfully over the course of the last 6 to 12 months, and in particular over the course of the last six to eight weeks. We could see silver up another 20%, down another 20%. It's not something that we are actively participating in in our fund.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about oil because, obviously, we're seeing some whipsaw action from day to day based on geopolitical tensions today. We're down almost 5% on a barrel of crude. How does an investor start to think about crude in this environment?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah so, we can address it in two parts. There's the fundamental supply-and-demand balance. And concurrently, we've got the geopolitical supply-side risk. Fundamentally, balances should be, on paper, over supply through 2026 in the tune of somewhere between 1 and 2 million barrels a day. This is something the market has been anticipating for a very long time.

Admittedly, the builds and inventories that we have seen globally have not occurred in commercial centers. So you're not seeing it in the Department of Energy data in the US. You're not seeing it in the ARA data out of Europe. You are seeing material builds and inventories, but those inventories are being held by China. It's gone into the Chinese SPR. And they do have room to continue to build, should they choose to do so.

We're also seeing a lot of oil sitting on water. This is part of the reflection of the sanctions. Oil has been stranded, particularly in the Far East. So you're seeing more oil sitting on water. Ultimately, that should come to market. But it's unclear if China, again, continues to buy given the geopolitical landscape. We've seen Venezuela disappear very much as a source for China.

Now, on the geopolitical front, and the reason that crude is rallying and has been rallying over the course of the last week, is right around the situation in Iran, which is a horrible situation-- fourth-largest producer of oil globally. They produce 3 million barrels a day, they export a million barrels a day. Unclear how that situation evolves, but the market is pricing in some risk around a supply disruption.

Greg, we've talked about this before. Over the course of the last 10, 12 years, we've not had any meaningful supply-side disruption around geopolitics. So I think the market's a bit benign on this, notwithstanding the fact that prices have increased. If there were to be a disruption, price would be materially higher than where it is today.

Greg Bonnell: You put all that together from an investor perspective, commodities have been grabbing a lot of headlines. What kind of role can they play in a portfolio?

Hussein Allidina: Yeah, so we've argued for a while. Academics and those that are students of portfolio construction will tell you that because commodities-- the derivative-- behaves differently than the stuff that we hold in our portfolio today-- equities, fixed income-- you want an allocation of 5% to 10% because it's diversifying, because it is one of the few assets that is positively correlated with inflation, so it acts as an inflation hedge.

And then on top of all that, Greg, given that the commodity space has not seen investment over the course of the last 10 to 15 years, we're in a very unique period for the commodity market where prices will have to move higher in an effort to attract capital. And again, we're competing today with AI that's taking a lot of that capital. AI itself is very commodity-intensive.

For the next five, seven years, we do see commodity returns averaging well above their long-term average of 5.4%. And we think that portfolios that have this 5-10% allocation to commodities will not only outperform those that don't, but the drawdown, historically, of a portfolio that has commodities is shallower than the portfolio that doesn't.

Original Post

data.title: IYK: Consumer Staples Dashboard For January
data.description:
This article offers a top-down view of the consumer staples sector based on value, quality, and momentum metrics. It may also help to analyze sector ETFs such as The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) and iShares US Consumer Staples ETF (IYK), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield (“EY”), Sales Yield (“SY”), Free Cash Flow Yield (“FY”), Return on Equity (“ROE”), Gross Margin (“GM”). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the “something” is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value And Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score (“VS”) is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score (“QS”) is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: Unusual Machines: A Speculative Bet Supported By Solid Fundamentals
data.description:
Thesis

A stock that you can not really tell if it is a fundamental investment or just speculation is Unusual Machines, Inc. (UMAC).

When they announced in November 2024 that Donald Trump Jr. had joined the company's advisory board, the stock went crazy. Its increase was sharp, with extreme volatility attracting investors and traders who are not afraid of risk.

The market overreacted even though nothing actually changed in the company's financial results. People just saw potential for political connections and strategic backing.

As I see it, the stock is moving based on expectations for the future and not on mature, proven fundamentals.

It is, therefore, a Buy bet on what the company can become and not on what it is today.

Today, with the stock trading near $15.5 and a market cap of almost $570 million, it looks really expensive.

Revenue for the first nine months of 2025 was just $6.3 million, leading to a Price to Sales ratio near 90x.

Why Traditional Valuation Is Not Enough

Unusual Machines is a company that does not have stable cash flows but is a growing business positioned in an industry with strong structural and geopolitical catalysts.

The market is not interested in today's numbers, as it is betting on what the company could become in a market that is reshaped by politics and new rules.

In such cases, traditional valuation ratios function more as an indicator of risk than as a forecasting tool.

According to me the real question is not if it is expensive now but if it can become an important player in America's drone supply chain.

That is why we need to look at valuation through different scenarios and not with one single forecast.

Business Model And Strategic Differentiation

Unusual Machines is a company that does not have stable cash flows but is a growing business positioned in an industry with strong structural and geopolitical catalysts.

The market is not interested in today's numbers, as it is betting on what the company could become in a market that is reshaped by politics and new rules. In such cases, traditional valuation ratios function more as an indicator of risk than as a forecasting tool.

According to me the real question is not if it is expensive now but if it can become an important player in America's drone supply chain.

That is why we need to look at valuation through different scenarios and not with one single forecast.

Business Model And Strategic Differentiation

Unlike many of its competitors, Unusual Machines does not build complete drones, but it focuses on producing critical components (motors, flight controllers, control electronics, cameras, FPV equipment).

Therefore, if you ask me the company plays in a different level and avoids direct competition with the companies that build the final products and instead of competing for market share it works as a Tier-1 supplier and can collaborate with many companies at once.

Regulations are probably the biggest driver of this investment story. The "NDAA" framework does not allow China and other unfriendly countries from supplying drones and parts to the U.S.

In addition, when FCC expanded these restrictions in 2024 things became even worse.

This is not temporary. The disconnection from Chinese supply chains in defense and technology is difficult to reverse.

As we see, this creates an environment where domestic suppliers compete not just on price, but on the ability to participate in the market itself.

The US drone market is experiencing significant acceleration in the military and in the civilian sector. The whole market becomes bigger as it expands in government, industrial and commercial sectors.

The Pentagon’s Replicator program want to produce hundreds of thousands of small, expendable drones by 2027.

Although these drones have a low unit cost, they rely on reliable, certified components.

For a components' supplier, the economic benefit comes from the volume and repeatability of orders.

As I see it, if Unusual Machines manages to establish itself as a reliable supplier in such programs, its growth profile could change drastically.

Financial Profile And Earnings Quality

Q3 2025 was the first profitable quarter of the company on a net basis. Its profit stood at $1.6 million.

However, this profit was derived from investment valuations and interest and not real business.

For now, revenue growth, margins and operating leverage are more important than actual profit.

The backlog of orders (more than $16 million) is the most significant element, as it offers visibility and the possibility of a sharp increase in turnover.

With more than $130 million in cash and zero debt, has a strong financial cushion. This gives it the time to invest, correct mistakes and implement its strategy without immediate pressure from the capital market.

Liquidity limits the risk of the negative scenarios but does not eliminate the risk of stock dilution in case of execution failure.

Production Capacity And Execution Risk

Despite the attractive story and strong catalysts, investing in Unusual Machines has serious risks you need to consider.

The most important risk is the execution risk. If the company will be able to successfully transition from limited production to mass to fulfill orders with reliability and consistency.

Many hardware companies fail at this point and not from lack of demand but from quality problems, delays and operations getting complicated.

Closely related to execution risk is the risk of losing or delaying contracts.

The way I see it, the investment case depends on government and military orders. These are known for their strict requirements, slow timelines and huge bureaucracy.

Another important risk is the increase in profit margins. While the parts model offers (theoretically) better gross margins, when you increase production, costs typically go up, both in raw materials and labor.

As we see, if the company does not keep costs under control, the operating leverage that the market expects may never come.

Furthermore, there is a risk of increased competition. The regulatory framework that currently favors Unusual Machines acts as an incentive for other players to invest in domestic production.

Lastly, we see the valuation risk.

At current prices, the stock already assumes a lot will go right. Any disappointment can lead to a sharp drop and volatility.

Looking at the stock, I will not only keep the positive performance and its persistence at historical highs.

I will focus on the high volatility (from $2 it shot to 15, from 15 it turned to 5, and moved back to 15, in a few months).

This behavior, although logical, shows that the investor who will buy should be prepared for fluctuations. Although I believe that it has a long way to go, it is not excluded that there will be significant impairments.

Scenario-Based Valuation

At its current price of $17.46, the company's market capitalization is close to $550 million and its enterprise value is close to $480 million.

From this point on, the stock's movement will be determined by whether specific operational and regulatory developments confirm or contradict the investment story.

In the positive scenario, the main value drivers are successfully scaling production, converting the existing backlog into recurring revenue and establishing the company as a core supplier of NDAA-compliant components.

This scenario would be reinforced by announcements of new multi-year contracts with government or military entities, expansion of partnerships with existing customers or participation in larger programs like Replicator.

At the same time, confirmation of stable or improving gross margins, along with signs of operating leverage, would be a strong signal that the business model can scale.

Under these conditions, revenue of $180 to $220 million and a market cap above $2 billion, translating to a price around $70 or 300% gain, would not be unreasonable by market standards.

In the base case scenario, the company keeps growing but at a slower pace. Production scales without major issues, but new contracts are smaller or more sporadic. Here, positive signs would be steady quarterly revenue growth, satisfactory backlog levels and no negative surprises on costs.

The market would view the company as growing but not a clear category winner, valuing it at an EV to sales around 8x. With revenue of $70 to $80 million, a price around $22-25% gain.

In the negative scenario, delays, quality problems and losing major contracts could hurt the investment.

If the company is not punctual or gets negative feedback from big customers, it loses credibility. The market would then cut the valuation significantly. With revenue around $40 million, the price would fall to $8.70, a 50% drop.

Conclusion

Unusual Machines is a pure speculative growth play with some fundamental basis but high execution risk.

The upside potential is big, but not guaranteed. For investors who can deal with high volatility and think long-term, it could justify a small, controlled position in the portfolio.

As far as I am concerned, the final outcome depends on execution, not the story or political names.

Can Unusual Machines management actually deliver consistent results? That's what counts.

data.title: Phoenix Education Partners: A High-Risk, High-Cash Bet In For-Profit Education
data.description:
Like a lot of you out there, I get a kick out of sniffing out new stocks.

While poking around in the American for-profit education world (a space that’s taken its lumps thanks to regulations and falling enrollments), I stumbled across Phoenix Education Partners, Inc. (PXED).

From up here, it kind of reminds me of other names I follow in the space like Strategic Education (STRA) and Grand Canyon (LOPE), and for good reasons too.

All three run a squeaky-clean balance sheet, stick to an asset-light model with minimal Capex, generate strong FCF, focus on flexible degrees, and above all, provide employable courses.

But here's where things really diverge.

GCE trades at a 12.6x EBITDA premium to the industry, Strategic sits just under at around 7x, yet Phoenix is ​​going for an eye-popping 5x EBITDA—especially after today’s double-digit tumble following its first quarterly results since last October’s IPO.

You don’t have to look too far to get why Phoenix trades at such a discount.

About 83.7% of the shares are still in the hands of PE firms, who have their own exit plans. That leaves a huge overhang and a tiny float—roughly 14% of shares, around 5 million.

Keep in mind, Apollo (APO) and Vistria are on their own clocks, and the IPO was really a backup plan after a failed ~$600 million sale to the University of Idaho in 2024.

Don’t worry if you haven’t heard this one yet. Pour yourself a coffee and let’s go back to the beginning.

From Founding To IPO

Most folks don’t realize this, but the University of Phoenix hasn’t always operated the way it does today.

It was founded in 1976 by John Sperling, built around serving working adults, and earned recognition from the Department of Education the following year.

By the 1990s, Apollo Education Group, which owns UoPX (it’s a different Apollo than Apollo Management), had already made its way to the public markets.

Then came the 2010s, when the company hit its stride, topping out at roughly 470,000 students—over five times what it has now—and close to $5 billion in revenue.

At this point, UoPX controlled ~500 campuses and learning locations, but ended up getting involved in a few bumps along the way:

  1. UoPX found itself in the crosshairs over claims of predatory recruiting. A few years later, the government pushed back, cutting its contracts at military bases in Europe through the Department of Defense, and the FTC piled on with an investigation into misleading advertising.
  2. By 2015, no college in the U.S. had students with more debt on their backs than UoPX, with the total climbing past $35 billion. In the 2010s, the Higher Learning Commission kept warning UoPX about weak academic output and a flawed business model, which ended up sending student acquisition costs through the roof.
  3. Rather than fixing the underlying issues, the company doubled down on advertising, spending millions, including more than $150 million, on stadium naming rights for what’s now State Farm Stadium.

At the end of the day, UoPX was still stuck with an outdated, capital-heavy model, and it eventually ended up being sold to the Apollo–Vistria consortium for ~$1.1 billion in 2017.

The playbook looked a lot like what Apollo had already run in other for-profit education deals, such as McGraw-Hill, BPP Holdings (which it picked up alongside Carlyle in 2009 and exited about five years later), and Global Schools Group.

Apollo would streamline the operations, trim the business down into a leaner setup—think asset-light ed-tech with strong cash generation—pull out special dividends along the way, and eventually sell the whole thing to the next owner.

In UoPX’s case, Apollo went to work fast, closing almost every campus but one, trimming staff, and refocusing marketing on career-driven programs.

The aim was to push FCF higher and tee up an exit in the years ahead.

And that almost played out in 2024, when Apollo tried to sell the business to the University of Idaho for roughly $600 million as a nonprofit. As I mentioned earlier, some legal hurdles got in the way, and suddenly, there weren't many clean options left for a straight-up sale.

That’s when the IPO became the fallback plan.

Apollo, together with Vistria, took the company public last year, but strictly through a secondary offering—no new shares, just a portion of their ownership being sold into the market.

To put some numbers on it, they raised about $136 million by selling roughly 4.25 million shares at $32 a pop, taking advantage of that brief thaw in the IPO market last year. UoPX came out with a valuation just north of $1.1 billion (nearly double the deal that fell apart in 2024) but the path to a full exit now looks a lot slower.

As things stand today, Apollo is still sitting on about 24.9 million shares (roughly 69% of the company and firmly in control) while Vistria holds another 4.9 million shares, or about 13.7%.

Put that together, and you're left with a float of only around 15% of the stock.

In other words, there's still plenty of overhang ahead as they work through their exit plans, not all that different from what we're seeing play out with Savers (SVV) and Ares.

The Business Model In A Nutshell

Like all for-profit education players, UoPX monetizes through tuition and course fees across undergrad, executive, and short programs.

Most of their students are enrolled online or in hybrid formats, which fits neatly with the working-adult crowd they cater to. A big chunk of revenue (roughly 88%) still flows from federal programs, so they’re operating fairly close to the guardrails.

Similar to Strategic Education, which owns Strayer, Capella, and several ed-tech platforms, the model is centered on employability. UoPX also partners directly with employers (about 32% of FY 2025 enrollments) and uses in-house AI platforms to match students with employers and build customized learning paths.

And when I say they run an asset-light model, I'm not exaggerating.

In the last twelve months alone, UoPX cranked out $152.5 million in OCF (roughly a stunning ~78% EBITDA-to-OCF conversion). With Capex barely moving the needle (remember, there’s only one physical campus left), FCF still came in at a hefty $131.1 million.

At a little over $1 billion in EV, this thing is throwing off a massive 13.1% FCF yield.

That easily tops Strategic (~7%), GCE (5.9%), and Laureate (LAUR) at roughly 4.5%.

Here’s a quick peek at the cash flow:

Even including recent one-off items (IPO-related costs and a cybersecurity incident), UoPX’s margins remain only modestly below the industry average, supported by its asset-light, digital-first ed-tech model.

This gap should normalize the temporary costs to roll off.

The Cash Cow Thesis

Put management’s revenue guide of $1.025 to $1.035 billion next to adjusted EBITDA of $244 to $249 million, and the math gets you to an adjusted EBITDA margin of roughly 25%.

Haircut that to roughly ~20% on an unadjusted basis, EBITDA would likely land somewhere in the $206 to $210 million range.

If EBITDA keeps converting to cash in the 75 to 80% range, OCF would land somewhere between $156 and $166.4 million. And assuming Capex stays steady at around ~$21 million, FCF would shake out in the $135 to $145.4 million range.

Sure, I’m glossing over operating assets here, but it already gives you a pretty good sense of the picture.

You can probably see where this is headed.

With a quarterly dividend of $0.21 per share ($0.84 annually, or about a ~2.7% yield) they’re set to return roughly $29.98 million a year to shareholders.

That’s just a touch over 20% of the low end of my FCF estimates.

I can easily see them nudging that up at a mid-to-high single-digit clip as Apollo lines up its exit, after having already pulled out a round of special dividends along the way.

And that’s where the story starts to get a little more interesting.

They may also be lining up new buyers to take down a larger block and finally make a clean exit.

With private equity likely losing interest in the asset, a strategic buyer could easily see value in the hybrid model and the B2B partnerships as a compelling angle here.

After all, at 3.6x forward EBITDA, the stock is trading at deeply discounted levels. Normalizing the multiple to a 5–6x adjusted EBITDA range implies an acquisition value of approximately $37.50 to $44.40 per share, representing at least a ~23% premium to the current share price.

OBBB: Headwind Or Tailwind?

On the last earnings call, the CEO went out of his way (even before the numbers came out) to underscore that UoPX remains fully compliant with all the requirements for Title IV funding, especially given the OBBB’s focus on accountability and workforce training.

As I flagged in my GCE piece, areas like IT and healthcare tend to do well when the focus is squarely on workforce outcomes, and UoPX seems to be leaning into that mix as well.

Even so, there are some clear risks on the table:

  1. Workforce Pell Grants are a real tailwind for schools built around short, technical programs. UoPX doesn’t get quite the same lift, though, since only a small portion of its revenue comes from that mix (only about ~10–15 certificates look adaptable or already short enough to qualify).
  2. As the graduate earnings bar keeps rising (miss the mark two out of three years and Title IV goes away) programs like behavioral sciences or liberal arts may not make the cut over time.
  3. The end of Grad PLUS and limits on Parent PLUS loans make it harder for certain students to tap into federal funding.

If you ask me, UoPX could tweak its portfolio toward high-employability areas, with OBBB providing some subtle support.

Adult education moving under the Department of Labor opens doors for better talent sourcing through upskilling, where employer partnerships really start to matter more than the old DOE rules.

Is It Worth The Investment?

To be perfectly clear, the Phoenix Education thesis isn’t really about margins or growth.

It’s about a distressed asset trading at rock-bottom multiples, with risks like regulatory pressure and Apollo’s overhang baked in.

The real question is whether their asset-light model, acting as a steady cash generator, is enough to offset all of that.

Looking at peers like Strategic (Strayer), GCE, Perdoceo (PRDO)—which owns Colorado Technical University and American InterContinental University—Adtalem (ATGE) with Chamberlain and Walden, and American Public Education (APEI), we get to an average EBITDA multiple of roughly 9x.

On that basis, Phoenix would screen at a price target of about $54.70 to $55.70.

That works out to about an 80% upside. In practice, though, that feels hard to underwrite once you layer in the regulatory risk and the Apollo overhang.

That said, I'd haircut that multiple by roughly 25% to 35% to build in a margin of safety, which brings it down to around 6.3x to 6.7x EBITDA. The stock screens at roughly $40 to $42.40 per share, still implying at least ~30% upside even at the low end.

Interestingly, even the Street ends up in the same neighborhood.

Graham’s model also backs this up, even after applying a slightly more conservative ~30% margin of safety (versus the 25% we used earlier). Using the Street’s FY 2026 EPS consensus of $4.40 and assuming a steady long-term growth rate of roughly 1.5% to 2%, the model points to a fair value range of about $40.30 to $43.80 per share.

For context, I'm assuming a historical AAA average yield of 5.31% versus a current yield of 4.66%.

If you really want to pressure-test it, a back-of-the-envelope DCF tells the same story. Take about $115 million of steady FCF, grow it at ~1.5% in perpetuity, discount it at around 9%, and you’re looking at roughly $42 per share.

I'm tempted to throw a Gordon model into the mix, but forecasting dividends this early in the cycle is probably a stretch.

Bottom line, I like Phoenix as a Buy at these levels, with the important caveat that this is a higher-risk name and deserves disciplined position sizing.

Outside of regulation (which is never too far from the surface in this space) and some possible OBBB headwinds, the one thing I’d really worry about is a rollover in enrollments.

And so far, that’s not showing up in the numbers.

In Q1 FY 2026, enrollments were up at least 4.1% year-over-year, comfortably outpacing the slower growth we’ve seen at peers like Strayer.

On the stock itself, as I mentioned earlier, this is very much a deep-value setup, with the added wrinkle of a low float and, as a result, higher volatility. This is the kind of position that belongs in a small sizing bucket, where the re-rating isn't driven by a single catalyst, but by how depressed the multiples already are.

After all, as I said before, it's a ~40% upside with a low single-digit yield annually.

data.title: Sealsq: The High-Growth Quantum Stock That The Market Is Overlooking
data.description:
The SEALSQ Investment Thesis

SEALSQ Corp. (LAES) continues to receive little attention, despite the fact that the company is growing rapidly and performing well. In the whole of 2025, there were only eight articles from five different authors. Competitors such as IonQ (IONQ) or Rigetti (RGTI) received many more articles. The daily volume of the stocks also speaks volumes.

  • SEALSQ: 7,938,488
  • IonQ: 14,263,531
  • D-Wave Quantum (QBTS): 26,207,549
  • Rigetti: 20,703,213.

SEALSQ continues to receive less attention than it deserves and remains a hidden gem in terms of growth. With a growth rate that could reach 100% next year, we are dealing with a hypergrowth company that is fairly valued, as its multiple is lower than that of its competitors.

But I am going to maintain a Hold rating, as I currently consider the shares to be fairly valued due to dilution from new offerings. For a Buy rating, the shares would need to be slightly cheaper.

What was my previous coverage like

So far, I have written two articles about SEALSQ. In my first article, I introduced the Swiss company and its business sectors. Currently, the company's focus is on secure data collection from sensors and devices, user identity security, and custom microcontrollers.

In the second article, I examined the results for the first nine months of 2025, as well as my assessment of what could realistically be achieved in 2026. As a lot has happened since my last article, I would now like to check whether my outlook for 2026 has changed fundamentally.

Preliminary results for FY25

SEALSQ presented a fantastic preliminary annual result with a 66% increase in sales. Since the 9-month results showed an increase in sales of 41%, it means that growth has accelerated significantly in the last quarter. The IC'ALPS acquisition seems to be bearing fruit, as it is a key contributor to the growth in sales. Therefore, I see it as a positive sign that SEALSQ continues to search for suitable M&A targets.

With Quobly SAS, a French company in the quantum computing sector, there are plans to first make a minority investment and then make a majority investment in the long term. If a majority stake were to be acquired, the deal would total approximately $200 million. In a press release from December last year, Quobly was referred to as a leader in the field of “Quantum Microelectronics” and would therefore fit well into the SEALSQ portfolio.

By embedding quantum-resistant security directly at the hardware level during the design phase, SEALSQ and Quobly intend to serve mission-critical sectors such as defense, intelligence, and financial services.

Source: Quantum Computing Report.

For now, this partnership sounds very exciting. However, as the quantum industry is still in its infancy, it is not yet possible to say whether it will be successful in the long term. Quantum remains risky, but also offers high potential returns.

Beyond the technical details, the Quobly–STMicro–Soitec collaboration reflects a distinctly European strategy—leveraging existing industrial strengths rather than attempting to outspend hyperscalers.

Source: EE Times.

Quobly is already working with the European semiconductor manufacturer STMicroelectronics (STM). But they are also aware that they lack the financial resources of their American competitors and must therefore take a different approach. But this collaboration shows that Quobly is a company with significant growth potential.

Valuation

I don't think a 28x P/S multiple for a company that grew revenues by 66% last year is too expensive. Especially since the company is still in the hypergrowth phase. Compared to its peers, the company is also much cheaper in terms of its multiple. Of course, this is also because SEALSQ is based in Switzerland, and U.S. investors tend to focus on American quantum stocks.

SEALSQ's market cap is currently at ~$850m, and the company has $425m in cash. So that's about half of the market cap in cash. If this is now used for M&A or invested in growth, I can imagine that growth could be strong in the next few years.

The Company reiterates its previously announced FY 2026 revenue growth of between 50% and 100% year-over-year.

Source: SEALSQ Press Release.

Previously, I estimated that the revenue for fiscal year 2026 would be between $26 million and $40 million. Due to the new guidance, however, I must now adjust my estimate. In my opinion, SEALSQ's guidance is still relatively conservative, so I think it will be exceeded. Personally, I would estimate growth to be between 75% and 110% for FY26.

$18 million FY25 revenue x 1.75 = $31.5 million.

$18 million FY25 revenue x 2.1 = $37.8 million.

This means that I have to raise my revenue estimate floor, but also have to limit the upside a bit. So, I consider revenues between $31.5 million and $37.5 million dollars to be realistic for FY26.

The Company’s expanding business pipeline now totals over $200 million in potential revenue opportunities from 2026 to 2028

Source: SEALSQ Press Release.

A few months ago, the pipeline was at $175 million, which means that orders were won. Assuming SEALSQ earns nearly $35 million in 2026, then $165 million would be left for 2027 and 2028. So it is not entirely unlikely that the company could surpass the $100 million mark in FY28. The analysts' revenue estimates for FY26 are currently at $34m, which aligns with my estimate.

So, I still consider the shares to be fairly valued.

Risks

A significant risk is the dilution caused by the offerings. The number of shares outstanding, which currently stands at 140 million, will probably increase significantly with the next results. As the FY25 results above are preliminary, YCharts has not yet updated them here, and we must wait for the final report. As there was a $200m offering in October and the cash reserves increased from $220m to $425m, I assume that dilution has occurred.

The company will raise $94.8 million through a registered offering of 12.64 million ordinary shares at $7.50 each and $105.2 million through a private placement involving pre-funded and Class D warrants.

The private offering consists of pre-funded warrants to purchase 14,026,666 ordinary shares and Class D warrants to purchase up to 53,333,332 ordinary shares at a combined offering price of $7.50.

Source: Seeking Alpha.

It will be interesting to see how many shares have been added in the next report, given that there are warrants and the offer price for the common stock was $7.50.

Conclusion

SEALSQ continues to fly under the radar despite its rapid growth. As a result, the multiple is reasonable compared to the peers, and the stock is fairly valued and not overvalued, unlike almost all competitors.

data.title: Sol-Gel Technologies: Downgrading To 'Hold' As Success Relies On Upcoming Q4 Catalyst
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Sol-Gel Technologies (SLGL), it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled “Sol-Gel Shines In Treating 2 Skin Disorders, NDAs Expected For Both Indications In 2020.” With respect to this article, I noted that the company was gearing up to receive FDA approvals for its drugs TWYNEO for acne vulgaris in adults and pediatric patients ages 9 and older and EPSOLAY for the treatment of inflammatory lesions for adults with Rosacea. Both of these drugs are alright but are no longer the key drivers of value for the company that investors should be looking at. A second update is that this company has been hard at work developing its topical hedgehog signaling pathway inhibitor SGT-610 for patients with Gorlin Syndrome.

The point of this drug is to block the mutation of the PTCH1 gene, which allows new tumors to grow. This particular candidate, helping this group of skin disorder patients, is already being evaluated in a phase 3 study. I bring this up because there is a major inflection point at the end of this year (Q4 of 2026), which would be the expected release of topline results from this late-stage study of SGT-610. It is an inflection point in the sense that, besides possibly generating shareholder value (if positive data is announced), it would also serve to allow the company to expand to another indication by the name of high-frequency basal cell carcinoma (BCC).

This is crucial because even though it is a rare disease, it is said that even if only going after severe forms of these BCC patients, it could double the market potential of SGT-610. Again, this other indication is not to be pursued until the results from the phase 3 study using this drug to treat patients with Gorlin Syndrome are known. With not much traction from ESPOLAY and TWYNEO over the years, plus the reliance on a major data readout from a phase 3 study in Q4 of 2026, I believe that I should downgrade Sol-Gel Technologies' stock to a “Hold” rating.

Q4 2026 Is Major Inflection Point For Sol-Gel As A Biotech

First and foremost, I believe that it is important to go over what Gorlin Syndrome is. But before that, it's important to highlight that should the company be able to somehow pull off a win in the phase 3 study using SGT-610 to treat patients with Gorlin Syndrome, then it believes that it can more than double the market potential for SGT-610 (even if it only goes after severe forms of BCC formations).

Gorlin Syndrome ((GS)), also known as nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome (NBCC), is a rare genetic disorder causing the formation of various benign and cancerous tumors. This autosomal dominant genetic disorder is caused by the inheritance of the tumor suppressor patched homolog 1 (PTCH1) gene. Why is a genetic mutation of this PTCH1 gene a bad thing? That's because it is important in stopping or preventing the smoothened receptor gene (SMO) from occurring. With the stoppage of this receptor gene, the Hedgehog signaling pathway is shut down when not needed and doesn't lead to BCC cell tumor cells dividing and growing uncontrollably. Thus, SGT-610 is a Hedgehog signaling pathway inhibitor that is responsible for not allowing the SMO receptor to lead to the formation of new BCCs in patients.

In order to see whether or not SGT-610 (Patidegib Gel 2%) is going to be effective in preventing the formation of BCCs on a patient's face with GS, the company is currently in the process of evaluating it in a phase 3 study. This late-stage trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 140 patients and then randomize them 1:1 to receive either of the following doses:

  • SGT-610 twice daily (BID dosing)
  • Vehicle Gel BID dosing

With everything I have described above, it comes down to the established primary efficacy endpoint of this study, which is to assess BCCs. That is, at the end of the 12-month period, there will be an assessment of the number of new BCCs formed between those who took SGT-610 and those who took Vehicle Gel. The downside to this program is that a clinical data readout is still very far away. This is because topline results from this phase 3 study, using this Hedgehog signaling pathway inhibitor to treat patients with GS, are not expected until Q4 of 2026. The company is first awaiting to see results from this study before deciding if it wants to move towards the expansion indication of HF-BCC. Should the phase 3 GS study meet the primary endpoint and establish positive data, then the company believes that it is going to be able to move forward with the expansion. With that being said, it is possible that it could initiate a phase 3 study using SGT-610 to treat patients with HF-BCC in 2027.

Financials

According to the 6-K SEC filing, Sol-Gel Technologies had cash, cash equivalents, and deposits of $6.8 million and marketable securities of $14.1 million as of September 30, 2025. This brings a total cash balance of $20.9 million as of this date. This is a problem in that even though the company has two approved products, it is not generating a lot of cash from them. The stock price has increased recently, recovering from past losses, but it was likely on the basis of the advancement of the phase 3 study of SGT-610 for the treatment of patients with GS. In terms of cash, the company is low, and I expect it to raise cash either before the release of phase 3 GS data or afterwards.

Either way, it has a cash crunch and needs to find new funds quickly. It is not just on something that I am projecting myself. This is something that the company notes itself, which is that it believes it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into Q1 of 2027. As far as TWYNEO and ESPOLAY go, the company had made agreements for these products with various companies across the globe. It is expected to launch them in many new territories in 2027 and 2028. Still, it does not expect to have a $10 million annual royalty stream of revenue for these products until 2031 and beyond. The company burns about $6.7 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $5.7 million in R&D expenses and then $1 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sol-Gel Technologies. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the ongoing evaluation of SGT-610 for the treatment of patients with Gorlin Syndrome. The thing is that the primary endpoint for this study is the number of new BCCs on the face at Month 12, comparing SGT-610 to vehicle gel. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the company's drug is going to do better than the vehicle at preventing the formation of new BCCs at month 12. Nor that the data to be released by the end of 2026 is going to allow it to be in a position to file for regulatory approval for it.

The second risk to consider would be that of the potential expansion opportunity of SGT-610, which is to possibly be used to treat patients with high-frequency BCC. The notion here is that there was high demand, and that since this drug could potentially work in treating Gorlin Syndrome, then it might also be effective in treating patients with high-frequency BCC. The risk here is that even though it has been given to a non-Gorlin patient in France with a severe form of high-frequency BCC, it doesn't automatically mean that it will work out well for many other patients. In addition, this expansion opportunity of targeting BCC is only to happen if the phase 3 study targeting Gorlin Syndrome patients with SGT-610 is positive. If a negative data readout happens, not only would it cause the company's stock to trade lower, but it would also mean no expansion into high-frequency BCC.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the low amount of cash on hand that it has. The reason why I'm stating this is because it has $20.9 million in cash as of September 30, 2025. Again, it believes that it is only going to be able to fund its operations into Q1 of 2027. This means it is going to have to find a way to raise cash in the next several months. This could include many things, like selling shares, taking on debt, issuing warrants, or other financial tools at its disposal. The only way that it could possibly avoid a cash raise is if it develops a partnership agreement with another company. However, this is not guaranteed to happen.

Conclusion

Sol-Gel Technologies has gained back some of its stock price that it lost since 2020. It is a promising biotech, but I think it's riskier than most others because there is not a sufficient number of backup pipeline products. If you look at its pipeline, it is quite bare, especially since it had failed to achieve positive results in a phase 1b study using SGT-210 for Darier disease. It might look at targeting other indications, but I believe it would only do so via investigator-initiated trials because it doesn't have a lot of cash on hand. Thus, everything for this company hinges on the phase 3 data in Q4 of 2026. If it hits, great, but if it misses, I'm going to give my honest opinion that it could end up filing for bankruptcy as the likely scenario.

data.title: Silver: My 2017 Thesis On Industrial Demand, Illustrates Why This Is Not A Silver Market Bubble
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It is easy to correlate the meteoric rise in silver prices with the dramatic rally in gold prices we saw in the past few years. There is a strong argument to be made, given that gold and silver prices have been moving in the same direction historically. Silver market dynamics are, in fact, very different from those of gold, because a rising share of silver demand comes from industrial applications, many of which do not allow for effective recovery of the silver content used in producing various products. The rising demand for industrial applications is coming close to matching total global mined output.

I wrote an article in early 2017 where I meant to illustrate just how much potential industrial demand there is on the planet, by pointing out that if the world were to hypothetically have to run exclusively on solar power, there would not be enough silver, already mined and above ground, as well as known reserves that are yet to be mined to make a world powered exclusively by solar power possible.

Because silver prices are up about fivefold since I wrote that article, and I expect to see further robust gains in the price of this metal, I want to revisit my reasoning behind continuing a buy & hold position, rather than take profits as I ordinarily tend to do when an investment accrues such gains. The main silver position in my stock portfolio is the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV), which is a convenient way to gain exposure to the silver market, on short notice, in reaction to increasingly volatile market moves. I am currently adding to my existing position on the dips, using my low initial entry point as leverage to manage downside risks.

About the SLV ETF

The iShares Silver Trust ETF is designed to match the performance of the silver spot price. It has an expense ratio of .5% and $41 billion in assets, in the form of 16,348 tonnes of silver.

As we can see from the chart, the ETF will slightly underperform the spot silver price in the longer term, due to factors such as the expense involved. There is an argument to be made in favor of making use of this ETF to conveniently adjust one's exposure to silver prices, and then exit and opt for buying physical silver with the proceeds.

A Brief Overview of My 2017 Article on Industrial Silver Demand

The article I wrote in 2017, entitled " Not Enough Silver To Power The World Even If Solar Power Efficiency Were To Quadruple," was meant to highlight how much silver we have available to satisfy the rising trend of using it in industrial applications, such as solar power, high-performance electronic circuits, and so on. I used the hypothetical model of having to run the world exclusively on solar power to help visualize what we can and cannot do given the geological endowment we live within.

I included hypothetical efficiency gains to make the exercise more realistic. The conclusion that we do not have enough silver to power the world on solar is not entirely consequential, because we do have other sources of energy, such as solar, hydro, nuclear, and, of course, hydrocarbons, that continue to dominate the global energy market. It does help to highlight the fact that we could potentially use up all the silver on the planet in industrial applications.

The importance of my inclusion of assumed efficiency gains into the calculations is not only to measure potential demand growth more accurately, but also to highlight the fact that the price of silver might not necessarily produce the demand destruction that is assumed to occur in certain industries, if the price of silver surges as it just did in the past year. While a significant price increase in any input will create some demand destruction pressures, the efficiency gains that were once cited as a reason to be bearish on silver prices are now dulling the demand destruction effect that higher silver prices are likely to have on industrial demand.

Silver's High Conductivity Properties Are Irreplaceable in a World of High Efficiency.

According to the Silver Institute, in 2024, silver demand in solar panels reached 198 million ounces, while demand stagnated in 2025. It was just under 100 million ounces in 2017, when I highlighted the demand potential for this particular industrial application for silver.

Total silver demand in electronics and electrical applications was 466 million ounces, meaning that solar panels accounted for about 40% of total demand in these fields. The non-photovoltaics demand came from applications that require relatively small amounts of silver, such as in smartphones, that use anywhere between 0.1 grams to 0.35 grams per unit. Just to get a sense of how silver prices might affect demand from smartphones, one gram of silver currently costs less than $3; therefore, most smartphones currently use less than $1 worth of silver. Laptops use about a gram of silver, making it a minor cost factor.

About 20 grams of silver is used in a solar panel, which may account for about 15% to 30% of the total cost of manufacturing, based on recent estimates. This is the one industry where I do see some potential for demand destruction as companies find solutions to use less, in response to the rising cost of silver. However, rising demand for solar power may negate the efficiency gains that companies are coming up with and implementing.

The drive to achieve optimal performance in most new technologies has been a driving force behind industrial adoption of silver use, despite it being far more expensive compared with other conductive materials such as copper. At the same time, mined production has been on a slight declining path in the past decade.

Global mined silver supplies were about twice as high as silver demand for industrial use. As of last year, mined output was 19% higher compared with industrial demand for the metal.

It is important to understand this aspect of the silver market dynamic. Most of the silver used in industrial applications tends not to be recovered. Once industrial demand surpasses mined production, we will enter a period where the net silver found above ground in various forms will start to decline. Its scarcity value is therefore set to increase, which suggests that the current silver market price is not only justified, but that we should expect further gains.

The path toward a market balance where mined silver output will not be enough to satisfy the world's growing appetite for the metal in industrial applications will not be linear. In other words, we should not base our timeline on the pace at which the gap narrowed since 2016. Market forces are likely to make the remaining gap harder to close. Higher silver prices should stimulate miners into investing more, while industrial consumers are likely to look for ways to save through more efficient use or outright full substitution where it is technically feasible.

Mined silver output growth potential is, in part, limited by the fact that silver is often produced as a byproduct of other metals, such as copper. In other words, if output in other industrial metals is not growing at a robust pace, neither will silver as a byproduct. There are mines where silver is the primary product, but those mines are increasingly grappling with lower-quality ores, and it is unclear whether higher prices might trigger interest in increasing output from limited resources, especially if miners believe that prices will trend higher from here. On the demand side, as I already illustrated, there are some industries where demand destruction can occur, such as the solar industry, but many industries, such as those in the electronics business, will not be affected by silver prices, to such an extent that it will erode their profitability, or not be able to pass on the costs to the consumer.

The Gold Rally May Not Be the Main Silver Price Catalyst.

It is often assumed that, due to silver's precious metal role, often referred to as the "poor man's gold", silver prices will move largely in the same direction as gold.

As the chart shows, the SLV ETF mostly moved in step with the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD) until more recently, when silver prices shot up dramatically, comparatively speaking.

This tells us that while there is a strong correlation between gold and silver prices, there are currently other silver-specific factors that are becoming the main drivers of the silver market. In other words, the gold market no longer leads the silver market. A decoupling occurred due to the silver market-specific fundamentals that I highlighted. It remains to be seen to what extent the two markets will remain decoupled and for how long. For now, silver is being pushed forward by the rising gold market, but it also benefits from the industrial demand factor, which, in my view, means it is set to outperform gold for the foreseeable future.

Investment Implications

Given the roughly 200% increase in the share price of the SLV ETF over the past year, the obvious dilemma arises as to whether one should consider taking profits. I have been asking myself this same question, carefully pondering the fundamentals. Looking back at my 2017 article, meant in part to illustrate the long-term industrial demand potential for silver, I do not see this as a market bubble, but rather as an adjustment to fundamental realities that have been building for a while. Global mined silver output declined by about 10% in the past decade, while industrial demand for silver increased by roughly 40%.

We are, in my view, less than a decade away from industrial demand for silver outpacing mined output permanently. In other words, from that point on, the total amount of silver stored in various forms, be it bricks or jewelry will start to decline, with most of it ending up being permanently lost in industrial processes. As the price of silver continues to rise, efforts will probably be made to retrieve more of it from electronic devices and other applications. We will still eventually see more silver consumed in industrial applications than miners will be able to produce, even after accounting for retrieval rates.

I last bought SLV shares at the end of December, taking advantage of a minor pullback in silver prices. I intend to continue adding more shares incrementally on any significant pullbacks, while being mindful of keeping my overall position at a gain level of over 100%. I am currently at about 140%. The way I see it, the odds of my overall SLV investment strategy losing money as long as I keep above that 100% level are low.

I am certain that we will see sizable pullbacks in silver prices in the coming months and years, which will rattle some investors, while others will see the opportunity. Given that the current mined silver output is largely absorbed by industry, I expect companies that require silver as an input to start stockpiling when the price declines. In other words, we may have permanently moved to a higher silver price level that became the new normal. Based on these considerations, I am in the camp that sees opportunity in a sizable pullback in silver prices, not among those who will see it as a risk.

Risks to my Thesis

There are plenty of potential pitfalls to my thesis. Silver demand can be curtailed due to several factors. The higher price might lead to a decline in jewelry demand and industrial demand through substitution. Higher prices may also spur higher mined output than expected. The supply/demand balance, especially when it comes to mined supply versus industrial demand, can be changed significantly, assuming that the higher price factor will have a higher impact than expected.

A return to central banks refocusing on shoring up the health and stability of fiat currency systems around the world might reverse the high demand for gold & silver as store of value mechanisms. I see very few indications that we are on the brink of sound fiat currency policies. Deficits around the world are rising, and central banks are accommodating. This, on its own, provides a higher price floor under silver prices in my view. The industrial demand situation is not likely to be reversed either.

Conclusions

The dramatic rise in the share price of the SLV ETF ordinarily would tend to raise questions on whether this may be the time to consider taking profits. In most cases, I tend to do just that, with questions on whether there may be more upside due to market momentum or other factors, leaving me holding just a minor position at most, while I tend to sell the bulk of such a lucrative investment.

In this particular case, the dramatic increase in share price is a reaction to a dramatic shift in fundamentals. We are clearly on our way to a silver market balance where industrial demand will surpass mined output, making the scarcity value dynamics of silver superior to that of gold, since most of the gold that is mined, adds to the volume of gold available above ground. We may be entering the era where the volume of silver above ground that is recognized and marketed as such will begin to shrink.

The concept I introduced in my article almost a decade ago, namely that there is more than enough potential industrial demand to consume all the known silver resources that the world was endowed with, above ground and below, is very relevant to the silver market situation today. It helps to illustrate the shift into a net deficit of mined output versus industrial demand dynamic, which will slowly start to devour the silver supplies that the world currently holds above ground to make up for the deficit.

I am not entirely certain when this might happen. We have come a lot closer to it in the past decade, and even though we are headed toward that shift at a slower pace now, due to higher silver prices slowing the convergence down, I believe there is a chance that we will hit that point as soon as the end of this decade. Within this context, silver prices are set to keep rising going forward, for the foreseeable future, making current silver investments a buy & hold, rather than an opportunity to take profits on the already sizable gains we experienced in the past year.

data.title: Why Is The U.S. Dollar So Strong To Start 2026? EUR/USD And Dollar Index Overview
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Despite what the banks and global analysts say in their 2026 projections, the dollar (DXY) has strengthened since the start of the year and shows only few signs of weakening.

Of course, we are just halfway through January; there is still plenty of time for things to change. However, the dollar's trajectory is now looking quite different.

The Dollar Index isn't at 110.00 like it was just a year ago, before the greenback corrected by 10% against all of its major peers - now hanging right around the 100.00 level.

Consolidating since mid-July, bears have mostly barked, but the harsh descent seems already over.

The reason for that? US economic performance is still beating its competitors despite tariffs, and US firms, particularly in the tech and AI sectors, are dominant.

Despite fears and headlines, American employment is still in a decent spot and has stalled its expected decline, as reflected in recent jobless claims and non-farm payroll numbers.

To add injury to insult, the US Federal Funds Rate is the highest among the majors (except for the Bank of England's Rate, with which it is tied), leaving basis trades well active.

It wasn't just the greenback that was targeted last year - currency debasement hit all majors, as seen in gold's performance against the OECD FX basket.

So, is the dollar still in danger? Yes.

Policy directives from the Trump Administration can be unpredictable for nations and investors - look at Canada PM Mark Carney turning to China for a Trade Deal in the absence of such from the US.

And of course, the Fed's Independence at stake keeps hurting the buck's prospects, notably fueling rallies in metals since August, but hasn't shown much change since, even with the latest Powell investigation from the DOJ.

But as long as commodities are still priced in US dollars, banks and firms still require dollar funding, and the US Treasury is still the most liquid, safe haven asset available, the dollar can't just disappear from the system - leading to the current market conditions.

Finally, with the recent interventions in Venezuela, threats to Iran, the dollar saw demand from Freedom Trade flows.

On the other hand, some technical signals could point to a short-term correction in USD demand.

We will look at the Dollar Index and EUR/USD to assess the current state of the market and whether more upside is warranted for the dollar after its strong, surprising start to 2026.

Dollar Index Daily Chart

The US dollar is rallying quite strongly since breaking out of its descending sequence from November.

Rebounding in a very consistent uptrend, DXY has held support and shown no corrections since December 24.

However, now reaching the highs of its 99.50 resistance zone, momentum is weakening, as can be seen on the Daily RSI.

Let's see what will tilt the scales on the 4H chart.

4H Chart and Levels of Interest

Showing a bear divergence in the 4H time frame RSI, combined with the reaction to the resistance level, it looks like the dollar is losing some steam.

Still, keep a close eye on the confluence of the 4H 50-MA at 98.96 and the 2026 channel lows.

  • Breaking that level would point to continued downside and could retest the December 2025 lows at 97.75.
  • In the event of any intervention in Iran, the dollar can also spike above, so keep that in mind.

Overall, the moving averages are flatlining in the US dollar, indicating further consolidation/range-bound action for the time being.

Levels of interest for the Dollar Index:

Resistance Levels

  • Immediate resistance 99.25-99.50
  • 2026 highs 99.49
  • 100.00 to 100.50 main resistance zone
  • 100.376 November highs

Support Levels

  • 98.96 4H 50-MA and channel lows
  • 98.50-98.80 intraday pivot zone
  • 98.00 key support (+/- 100 pips)
  • December lows 97.75
  • 97.40-97.80 August range support
  • 2025 lows 96.40-96.80 support

EUR/USD 4H chart and Technical Levels

EUR/USD is showing reactions corroborating with the slowing momentum in the DXY.

Bouncing from the 1.16 support and channel lows, mean reversion should hold well towards the 4H 50-MA at 1.1655.

Rallying on high volume and candles could easily lead to a breakout of the descending channel towards the flatlining 200-period moving average (at 1.17).

Levels to place on your EUR/USD charts:

Resistance Levels

  • 1.1630-1.1670 Pivot zone (channel highs and 4H 50-MA)
  • 1.17 4H 200-MA
  • 1.1750 mini-resistance
  • Resistance Zone around 1.18 (+/- 150 pips)

Support Levels

  • 1.1580-1.16 key support
  • 1.1560 channel lows
  • 1.1470-1.15 pivotal support
  • 1.1350-1.14 support
  • Session lows 1.1593

Safe Trades, and keep a close eye on weekend risk and headlines!

Original Post
